content.text: A discriminative model learns to predict an output y directly from an input x by modelling the conditional distribution p(y | x). Rather than asking "how was this data generated?", it asks "given this input, what is the most likely label?" This narrow focus on the decision boundary — rather than the full data distribution — makes discriminative models highly effective for classification and regression tasks, and they are the dominant paradigm in supervised learning.
A discriminative model parameterises p(y | x; θ) directly. For binary classification with labels y ∈ {0, 1}, logistic regression is the canonical example:
For multi-class classification with y ∈ {1, …, K}, the standard choice is a softmax over K linear projections:
The key point is that neither formula requires knowing p(x) — the marginal distribution of inputs. The model focuses entirely on the conditional relationship between x and y.
Given a labelled dataset {(x_i, y_i)}_{i=1}^n, training maximises the conditional log-likelihood:

This is equivalent to minimising the cross-entropy loss, which measures how well the predicted distribution over labels matches the true label for each example. The model is never asked to assign probability to x itself — only to y given x. This allows discriminative models to ignore irrelevant variation in the input space and focus entirely on features that separate classes.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch import Tensor


class DiscriminativeClassifier(nn.Module):
    """
    A discriminative classifier that directly models p(y | x).
    The forward pass outputs a conditional distribution over K classes
    given input x — it makes no attempt to model p(x).
    """
    def __init__(self, input_dim: int, n_classes: int, hidden_dim: int = 256):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, n_classes),   # logits for each class
        )

    def forward(self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        return self.net(x)   # (B, K) unnormalised logits

    def predict_proba(self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        """Returns p(y | x) as a proper probability distribution."""
        return torch.softmax(self.forward(x), dim=-1)   # (B, K)

    def predict(self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        """Returns argmax_y p(y | x): the most likely class."""
        return self.forward(x).argmax(dim=-1)   # (B,)


def train_step(
    model: DiscriminativeClassifier,
    optimizer: torch.optim.Optimizer,
    x: Tensor,
    y: Tensor,  # ground-truth class indices (B,)
) -> float:
    """One gradient step on the conditional log-likelihood (cross-entropy)."""
    logits = model(x)                           # (B, K)
    loss = nn.functional.cross_entropy(logits, y)  # -log p(y | x) averaged
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    return loss.item()

The defining property of a discriminative model is that it learns a decision boundary in input space — the surface where p(y = 1 | x) = 0.5 — rather than the full data distribution. In a 2-class problem, logistic regression finds a linear hyperplane; deep neural networks find complex nonlinear boundaries. The model can be highly accurate in the regions where training data is dense and completely undefined in regions with no training examples — this is acceptable because the model is never asked to generate or evaluate the plausibility of new x values.

This also explains why discriminative models are vulnerable to out-of-distribution inputs: a classifier trained to separate cats from dogs may confidently misclassify a noise image as one or the other, because nothing in its training objective prevents it from assigning high probability to nonsensical inputs.
Why Discriminative Models Usually Win at Classification

Given the same number of parameters, discriminative models tend to outperform generative models on classification benchmarks. The theoretical explanation (Vapnik's principle): if you want to predict y from x, solve that problem directly — do not solve the harder problem of modelling p(x, y) as an intermediate step. Modelling p(x) requires handling all the variation in the input space (pose, lighting, texture, etc. for images), which consumes capacity that would be better spent on the classification boundary.

Empirically, this plays out in two ways. First, discriminative models reach higher accuracy with less data in the supervised regime. Second, they converge faster in training because the loss signal is always label-aligned — every gradient step directly improves the mapping from x to y, rather than also trying to reconstruct or model x.
The Asymmetry of the Conditional Distribution
A discriminative model p(y | x) is inherently asymmetric: it produces a distribution over y given x, but not over x given y. This has practical consequences:
Cannot generate new inputs x ~ p(x | y). A cat-dog classifier cannot produce a new image of a cat.
Cannot handle missing features without imputation — p(y | x) requires all features of x to be present (or must be redesigned to marginalise over missing values).
Cannot detect out-of-distribution inputs — the model assigns p(y | x) for any x, including nonsensical ones, with no built-in mechanism to flag that x is implausible.
Cannot be used for density estimation or anomaly detection without augmenting with a separate p(x) model.

Discriminative models extend naturally to structured output spaces where y is a sequence, tree, or graph rather than a single class label. Sequence-to-sequence models, conditional random fields, and object detectors are all discriminative: they model p(y | x) where y is an entire sequence of tokens, a sequence of named-entity tags, or a set of bounding boxes.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch import Tensor


class Seq2SeqDiscriminative(nn.Module):
    """
    Sequence-to-sequence discriminative model: directly models p(y | x)
    for structured output y = (y_1, ..., y_T) given input sequence x.
    An encoder-decoder Transformer is this type — it predicts the next token
    conditioned on the source x and all previous target tokens (teacher forcing).
    """
    def __init__(self, vocab_size: int, d_model: int = 256, n_heads: int = 4):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = nn.TransformerEncoder(
            nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(d_model, n_heads, batch_first=True),
            num_layers=3,
        )
        self.decoder = nn.TransformerDecoder(
            nn.TransformerDecoderLayer(d_model, n_heads, batch_first=True),
            num_layers=3,
        )
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, d_model)
        self.out = nn.Linear(d_model, vocab_size)

    def forward(self, src: Tensor, tgt: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        """
        src: (B, S) source token ids
        tgt: (B, T) target token ids (teacher-forced)
        returns: (B, T, vocab_size) logits for p(y_t | x, y_<t)
        """
        memory = self.encoder(self.embed(src))    # (B, S, d_model)
        out = self.decoder(self.embed(tgt), memory)  # (B, T, d_model)
        return self.out(out)                       # (B, T, vocab_size)

    def conditional_nll(self, src: Tensor, tgt: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        """
        Negative log-likelihood: -log p(y | x) = sum over t of -log p(y_t | x, y_<t)
        This is the discriminative training objective.
        """
        logits = self.forward(src, tgt[:, :-1])   # (B, T-1, V)
        targets = tgt[:, 1:]                       # (B, T-1) shifted right
        return nn.functional.cross_entropy(
            logits.reshape(-1, logits.size(-1)),
            targets.reshape(-1),
        )
Discriminative vs Generative: When Each Wins
The Boundary Blurs: Discriminative Pre-training + Fine-tuning

Modern practice often combines both paradigms. BERT is pre-trained generatively (masked language modelling: predict masked tokens, which is a discriminative objective over the vocabulary conditioned on context) and then fine-tuned discriminatively (a classification head on top). GPT models use a purely generative pre-training (autoregressive language modelling) and then a discriminative fine-tuning (RLHF, instruction tuning) to align them to user intent. The distinction is most useful as a conceptual framework for understanding what a model can and cannot do, rather than as a rigid architectural classification.

Mental model: a discriminative model is a judge that has studied thousands of court cases and learned to rule confidently on any case presented to it — but cannot write a story about what happened. A generative model is a novelist who understands how events unfold and can imagine new scenarios — but is not necessarily better at classifying existing ones. The judge (discriminative) is better at its narrow task precisely because it has never had to learn to write.

A discriminative model consists of two components: an encoder f_θ(x) that maps the input into a representation h, and a prediction head g_φ(h) that maps the representation to a distribution over output labels. During training, labeled examples {(x_i, y_i)} flow through the model, the cross-entropy loss is computed against the true label, and gradients update both the encoder and head via backpropagation. The model learns to separate the classes in representation space without ever needing to model the density of the inputs themselves.

attrs.alt: Discriminative model architecture: input goes through encoder then prediction head to produce p(y|x), trained with cross-entropy loss on labeled data.

content.text: The encoder can be any differentiable function: a convolutional network for images, a transformer for sequences, or a simple MLP for tabular data. The head is typically a linear layer followed by a softmax normalizer. The critical architectural choice is that neither component is asked to model p(x) — the entire network capacity is devoted to the conditional mapping from input to label, which is why discriminative models outperform generative ones in label-efficient classification regimes.

By solving only the prediction problem p(y|x), the model does not waste capacity on p(x). Benchmark results consistently show discriminative models outperforming generative counterparts with the same parameter budget when labels are available.

Cross-entropy loss is convex in the softmax output, well-understood, and amenable to large-batch stochastic gradient descent. There is no need for MCMC, variational inference, or adversarial training — all of which introduce instability or tuning overhead.
Every gradient step directly improves the label prediction — there is no off-target gradient signal from trying to model the input. This translates to fewer epochs needed to reach peak accuracy.

Softmax outputs from a well-trained discriminative model can be calibrated with Platt scaling or temperature scaling to produce reliable confidence scores, which is critical for decision thresholding in production.

Adding a classification head to a pretrained encoder (BERT, ResNet, CLIP) and fine-tuning discriminatively requires minimal engineering and achieves strong results even with hundreds of labeled examples.
Cannot generate or sample from the input space.

A discriminative model has no access to p(x) and therefore cannot produce novel instances x ~ p(x | y). Tasks like data augmentation, conditional image synthesis, or anomaly scoring via log-density require a generative model or a separate density estimator.

The model assigns p(y|x) to every input, including inputs far outside the training distribution. Without a separate OOD detector, a classifier can output high-confidence wrong predictions on adversarial or shifted inputs.

The conditional likelihood objective requires (x, y) pairs. Unlabeled data is unused unless augmented with semi-supervised techniques (pseudo-labeling, consistency regularization) that add complexity.

p(y | x) requires all features of x. Marginalizing over missing features requires imputation or specialized architectures (e.g., MIWAE, missForest), unlike generative models which can marginalize p(x) naturally.
How to Implement a Discriminative Classifier

The following walkthrough implements a complete discriminative text classifier using a pretrained BERT encoder and a linear classification head. It covers the full pipeline: tokenization, model definition, training loop with early stopping, and probability calibration.

from __future__ import annotations
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder
from sklearn.calibration import CalibratedClassifierCV
import numpy as np


# ── 1. Data preparation ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
texts = [
    "The product arrived damaged and customer support was unhelpful.",
    "Absolutely love this item, exceeded all expectations!",
    "Average quality, nothing special but gets the job done.",
    "Terrible experience, will never buy again.",
    "Great value for money, fast shipping too!",
]
labels = ["negative", "positive", "neutral", "negative", "positive"]

le = LabelEncoder()
y = le.fit_transform(labels)               # [1, 2, 0, 1, 2] (sorted alphabetically)
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(texts, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)


# ── 2. Tokenization ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

def tokenize(texts: list[str], max_length: int = 128):
    return tokenizer(
        texts,
        padding=True,
        truncation=True,
        max_length=max_length,
        return_tensors="pt",
    )

train_enc = tokenize(X_train)
val_enc   = tokenize(X_val)

train_ds = TensorDataset(train_enc["input_ids"], train_enc["attention_mask"], torch.tensor(y_train))
val_ds   = TensorDataset(val_enc["input_ids"],   val_enc["attention_mask"],   torch.tensor(y_val))
train_dl = DataLoader(train_ds, batch_size=2, shuffle=True)
val_dl   = DataLoader(val_ds,   batch_size=2)


# ── 3. Model definition ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class BertDiscriminativeClassifier(nn.Module):
    """
    Discriminative model: BERT encoder + linear classification head.
    Directly parameterises p(y | x; θ, φ) — never models p(x).
    """
    def __init__(self, n_classes: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = AutoModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
        hidden = self.encoder.config.hidden_size       # 768 for bert-base
        self.dropout  = nn.Dropout(dropout)
        self.head     = nn.Linear(hidden, n_classes)   # discriminative head

    def forward(self, input_ids, attention_mask):
        # Encoder: bidirectional transformer, produces contextual embeddings
        enc_out = self.encoder(input_ids=input_ids, attention_mask=attention_mask)
        cls_repr = enc_out.last_hidden_state[:, 0, :]  # [CLS] token
        return self.head(self.dropout(cls_repr))        # (B, n_classes) logits

    def predict_proba(self, input_ids, attention_mask) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Returns p(y | x) — calibrated probabilities summing to 1."""
        return torch.softmax(self.forward(input_ids, attention_mask), dim=-1)


n_classes = len(le.classes_)
model = BertDiscriminativeClassifier(n_classes=n_classes)
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=2e-5, weight_decay=0.01)
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()   # maximises conditional log-likelihood


# ── 4. Training loop with early stopping ──────────────────────────────────────
best_val_loss = float("inf")
patience = 3
no_improve = 0

for epoch in range(10):
    model.train()
    for input_ids, attn_mask, labels_batch in train_dl:
        logits = model(input_ids, attn_mask)
        loss = criterion(logits, labels_batch)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
        optimizer.step()

    # Validation
    model.eval()
    val_loss = 0.0
    with torch.no_grad():
        for input_ids, attn_mask, labels_batch in val_dl:
            val_loss += criterion(model(input_ids, attn_mask), labels_batch).item()

    if val_loss < best_val_loss:
        best_val_loss = val_loss
        torch.save(model.state_dict(), "best_discriminative.pt")
        no_improve = 0
    else:
        no_improve += 1
        if no_improve >= patience:
            print(f"Early stopping at epoch {epoch}")
            break

# ── 5. Inference ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
model.load_state_dict(torch.load("best_discriminative.pt"))
model.eval()
test_enc = tokenize(["This exceeded my expectations!"])
with torch.no_grad():
    probs = model.predict_proba(test_enc["input_ids"], test_enc["attention_mask"])
    pred_class = probs.argmax(dim=-1).item()
print(f"Prediction: {le.inverse_transform([pred_class])[0]}")
print(f"Confidence: {probs[0, pred_class]:.3f}")

A production sentiment classifier at an e-commerce company typically receives millions of reviews per day. The discriminative approach is preferred over generative models here for two concrete reasons: inference speed (no sampling loop) and accuracy (labels are abundant from star ratings). The following shows a realistic serving setup that adds temperature scaling for calibration and batch inference for throughput.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List


@dataclass
class SentimentResult:
    label: str
    probability: float
    raw_logits: list[float]


class TemperatureScaledClassifier(nn.Module):
    """Wraps a discriminative classifier with learned temperature scaling.

    Temperature scaling: after training, fit a single scalar T on a held-out
    calibration set to minimise cross-entropy, then divide logits by T at
    inference. This does not change predictions (argmax is temperature-invariant)
    but makes the confidence scores reliable — e.g. 80% confident really means
    ~80% of those predictions are correct.
    """
    def __init__(self, base_model: nn.Module, temperature: float = 1.0):
        super().__init__()
        self.model = base_model
        self.temperature = nn.Parameter(torch.tensor([temperature]))

    def forward(self, input_ids, attention_mask):
        logits = self.model(input_ids, attention_mask)
        return logits / self.temperature   # calibrated logits

    def fit_temperature(self, val_dl, n_steps: int = 100, lr: float = 0.01):
        """Learn optimal T on a calibration (validation) set."""
        optimizer = torch.optim.LBFGS([self.temperature], lr=lr, max_iter=n_steps)
        all_logits, all_labels = [], []
        with torch.no_grad():
            for ids, mask, labels in val_dl:
                all_logits.append(self.model(ids, mask))
                all_labels.append(labels)
        logits_all = torch.cat(all_logits)
        labels_all = torch.cat(all_labels)

        def step():
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            loss = nn.functional.cross_entropy(logits_all / self.temperature, labels_all)
            loss.backward()
            return loss

        optimizer.step(step)
        print(f"Calibrated temperature: {self.temperature.item():.3f}")


class SentimentService:
    """Production-ready discriminative sentiment classifier with batch inference."""

    LABELS = ["negative", "neutral", "positive"]

    def __init__(self, model_path: str, device: str = "cpu"):
        self.tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
        # Load calibrated model
        self.model = torch.jit.load(model_path) if model_path.endswith(".pt") else None
        self.device = device

    def predict_batch(self, texts: List[str], batch_size: int = 64) -> List[SentimentResult]:
        results = []
        for i in range(0, len(texts), batch_size):
            batch = texts[i: i + batch_size]
            enc = self.tokenizer(
                batch, padding=True, truncation=True,
                max_length=256, return_tensors="pt"
            )
            with torch.no_grad():
                logits = self.model(**enc)          # calibrated logits
                probs  = torch.softmax(logits, -1)  # p(y | x)
            for prob_row, logit_row in zip(probs, logits):
                pred_idx = prob_row.argmax().item()
                results.append(SentimentResult(
                    label=self.LABELS[pred_idx],
                    probability=prob_row[pred_idx].item(),
                    raw_logits=logit_row.tolist(),
                ))
        return results
Treating softmax confidence as a reliability score without calibration.

Softmax outputs are systematically over-confident on held-out data. A model that outputs 0.98 probability may be correct only 80% of the time. Always fit temperature scaling or Platt scaling on a calibration set before deploying confidence thresholds.
Using a discriminative model for anomaly detection without modification.

The model assigns p(y|x) to every x, including noise inputs that are nothing like the training distribution. Add an OOD detector (Mahalanobis distance on encoder features, energy-based scores, or a separate density model) before relying on the classifier on open-domain inputs.
Ignoring class imbalance in the cross-entropy loss.

If 95% of examples are class 0, the model minimizes cross-entropy by predicting class 0 always. Use weighted cross-entropy (weight proportional to inverse class frequency), focal loss, or oversample minority classes.
Fine-tuning with a learning rate that is too high.

A learning rate above 1e-4 for a pretrained transformer head causes catastrophic forgetting of the encoder representations. Use a learning rate of 1e-5 to 5e-5 for the full model and 1e-3 or higher only for the classification head.
Evaluating on the test set during model selection.

Using test-set accuracy to select hyperparameters (number of layers, learning rate, dropout) introduces selection bias and overstates generalization. Use a held-out validation set for all model selection and reserve the test set for a single final evaluation.

After training, fit temperature scaling on a held-out validation set. It is a one-parameter optimization that takes seconds and makes probability outputs meaningful for downstream decision thresholding.

Clip gradient norms to 1.0 for transformer-based discriminative models. This prevents the occasional large gradient from destabilizing pretrained representations, which is especially important in the first few training epochs.
Monitor both accuracy and calibration error.

Expected Calibration Error (ECE) is the average gap between confidence and accuracy across confidence bins. A well-calibrated discriminative classifier should have ECE < 5% — if it is higher, apply calibration before deployment.
Layer-wise learning rate decay for fine-tuning.

Apply a smaller learning rate to earlier (more general) encoder layers and a larger rate to later (more task-specific) layers and the classification head. A typical decay factor is 0.9 per layer from the top.

Inference complexity for a discriminative model with encoder depth L and sequence length T is O(L · T² · d) for transformer-based encoders (due to the attention mechanism's quadratic cost in sequence length) plus O(d · K) for the classification head with d hidden dimensions and K classes. Throughput scales linearly with batch size up to memory limits — a BERT-base model running on a single A100 GPU achieves roughly 1,500 to 2,000 examples per second at sequence length 128 with batch size 128.

Key optimization techniques for production discriminative classifiers include: (1) quantization (INT8) which typically reduces memory by 4x and improves throughput 1.5–2x with less than 1% accuracy drop; (2) knowledge distillation to a smaller student model (e.g., DistilBERT achieves 97% of BERT's accuracy at 60% of its size and 2x inference speed); (3) ONNX export and TensorRT compilation for up to 3–5x additional throughput improvement over PyTorch eager mode; and (4) dynamic batching at the serving layer to maximize GPU utilization across variable-length requests.
Dedicated calibration library for discriminative models

Discriminative models face two distinct classes of failure in production. The first is distributional shift: when the input distribution p(x) at inference time differs from the training distribution, the conditional distribution p(y|x) learned during training may be inaccurate. This is not detected by the model itself — a spam classifier trained on 2022 email patterns may confidently misclassify 2025 spam that uses different vocabulary. The second class is adversarial vulnerability: because discriminative models only model the boundary and not the density, small structured perturbations to x can cross the decision boundary while being imperceptible to humans.

Track input feature statistics (mean, variance, token distribution) in production and alert when they diverge from training statistics. Libraries like Evidently, WhyLogs, and Alibi Detect provide drift detection for discriminative classifiers.
Deploy OOD detection alongside the classifier.
The energy score (sum of exp(logits)) or the Mahalanobis distance from class means in feature space are reliable OOD indicators that add only microseconds of inference cost.
Apply adversarial training for high-stakes tasks.

For fraud detection, content moderation, and security-critical classifiers, augment training with adversarial examples generated by FGSM or PGD. This measurably improves robustness to both intentional attacks and naturally occurring edge cases.
A discriminative model directly parameterises p(y | x; θ), optimised by maximising conditional log-likelihood (equivalent to minimising cross-entropy).
It learns a decision boundary — not a data distribution — which makes it more data-efficient for supervised classification than generative alternatives.
The trade-off is inability to generate new inputs x, handle missing features naturally, or detect out-of-distribution data without supplementary mechanisms.
The class spans logistic regression, SVMs, CNN/MLP classifiers, fine-tuned BERT, CRFs, and seq2seq models — any model whose training objective is directly p(y | x).
In modern LLM practice, the discriminative/generative boundary blurs: generative pre-training followed by discriminative fine-tuning (RLHF, instruction tuning) is the dominant pipeline.
